text:
Diabetic nephropathy (DN) is one of the most serious complications of diabetes and is a major cause of end-stage renal disease. Rhein, a bioactive anthraquinone derivative, has been reported to exert beneficial effects on kidney disease. However, the underlying molecular mechanisms remain unclear. The present study aimed to investigate the effects of Rhein on podocyte injury and renal fibrosis in vitro using a high glucose (HG) stimulated podocyte model and in vivo using streptozocin (STZ) induced diabetic nephropathy (DN) mice.

Our data showed that Rhein treated podocytes showed higher viability and resistance to HG-induced injury. Rhein suppressed ferroptosis by regulating lipid ROS, MDA, Fe2+ levels in cells. TFR1 and ACSL4 expression was decreased, while GPX4 and SLC7A11 expression was increased. Rhein suppressed the EMT process in podocytes by downregulating α-SMA, Vimentin, Snail and Desmin expressions, and upregulating Nephrin expression.

Rhein inhibited the activation of Rac1 and subsequently suppressed the expression of NOX1 and β-catenin. The in vivo caspase activity inhibition assay further confirmed that Rhein inhibited ferroptosis through inactivating Rac1. Rac1 siRNA mimicked the protective effects of Rhein, while oe-Rac1 and LV-Rac1 abolished the Rhein-mediated inhibition of ferroptosis and EMT-driven fibrosis in vivo. Rhein treated DN mice showed improved renal pathological structure, reduced fibrosis in the tubulointerstitium and lower levels of serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen.

Rhein suppressed the progression of diabetic nephropathy (DN) through non-ferroptotic inhibition of autophagy and the epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) process by inactivating the Rac1/NOX1/β-catenin signaling pathway. This study revealed a robust molecular rationale for using Rhein as a specific therapeutical reagent for DN.
